model_output_raw_text: {"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"DEPTH = 2","new":"DEPTH = 3","anchor":"","text":"","source":""},{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 12","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","anchor":"","text":"","source":""},{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"CHANNEL_MULT = 2","new":"CHANNEL_MULT = 1","anchor":"","text":"","source":""},{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 48","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 128","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The current flat MLP is small for CIFAR-10. Increasing depth modestly and widening to a stable constant-width hidden stack should improve representational capacity within the same verifier budget while keeping the training loop and optimizer unchanged.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 64","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"With a fixed 256-step budget, the current setup only processes a small fraction of the training set. Increasing batch size is a simple way to expose the model to substantially more examples within the same step budget, which is especially relevant for a flat MLP trained on CPU.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 5e-4","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The larger batch improved validation loss substantially, which suggests the run was sample-limited under the fixed 256-step budget. A higher Adam learning rate is a simple next move to accelerate optimization at the new batch size without changing model or loop behavior.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 5e-4","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The batch-size increase was the strongest improvement, while the later higher learning rate degraded loss. Keeping the budget-aligned cosine schedule but restoring the smaller learning rate is a clean way to test whether the schedule helps when the base step size remains in the better-performing range.","secondary_modes":["summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"summaries","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"USE_LR_SCHEDULE = True","new":"USE_LR_SCHEDULE = False","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"summaries","rationale":"The budget-aligned cosine schedule consistently moved loss in the wrong direction relative to the strong batch-size-only run. Turning it back off cleanly restores the better optimization regime and avoids spending more steps on a scheduler that has not helped this workload.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The main gain so far came from increasing batch size under the fixed 256-step budget, which effectively increased dataset coverage. Pushing batch size further is a simple continuation of the same successful idea and may improve optimization by exposing the model to even more examples within the same step limit.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 1024","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Batch scaling has been the clearest win on this fixed-step workload, and the 512-batch run improved substantially again. Testing 1024 continues the same effective strategy by maximizing training-set coverage per optimizer step while keeping the script otherwise unchanged.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 1024","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 2048","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Larger batch sizes have produced the strongest and most consistent gains on this fixed-step workload, and the 1024 run improved again. Increasing to 2048 is the simplest next extension of the same winning pattern, maximizing sample coverage per optimizer step without changing any other behavior.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 5e-4","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The run now uses a much larger batch size, and large-batch optimization often benefits from a larger Adam step size. This is a focused scalar follow-up to the successful batch-scaling trend, with a better chance of helping here than it had at smaller batch sizes.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"OPTIMIZER = \"adam\"","new":"OPTIMIZER = \"adamw\"","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"The current regime is effectively large-batch, multi-epoch optimization within a fixed-step budget. Switching from Adam to AdamW is a compact optimizer-family change that often improves generalization under this kind of setting without disturbing the successful batch size and learning rate choices.","secondary_modes":["caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"OPTIMIZER = \"adamw\"","new":"OPTIMIZER = \"adam\"","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"The AdamW switch slightly worsened validation loss while the prior Adam configuration was the best observed with this batch size and learning rate. Reverting to Adam restores the stronger optimizer regime before exploring further changes.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 0.0","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"After scaling both batch size and learning rate, the model is training much more aggressively and seeing the data many times within the fixed-step budget. Removing weight decay is a simple regularization test to check whether the current decay is now underfitting the best-performing regime.","secondary_modes":["indexing"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 24","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The optimization-side changes appear near a local plateau, which suggests model capacity may now be the bottleneck. Increasing the hidden width moderately is a focused architecture test that preserves the current successful training regime while giving the flat MLP more representational power.","secondary_modes":["micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 0.0","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The wider network improved validation loss, which makes it worth revisiting mild regularization under the new capacity regime. Restoring a small weight decay is a simple way to test whether the larger MLP benefits from a bit more constraint without disturbing the successful batch size, width, or learning rate choices.","secondary_modes":["layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 128","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 512","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The moderate width increase helped, suggesting the model is still capacity-limited. Raising the minimum hidden width to 512 is a simple architecture follow-up that enlarges all hidden layers without changing the training loop or introducing more structural complexity.","secondary_modes":["caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 0.0","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The wider hidden layers improved validation loss, and the earlier zero-weight-decay runs were competitive before this extra capacity increase. Re-testing zero weight decay in the stronger architecture is a compact way to check whether the current regularization is now slightly over-constraining the model.","secondary_modes":["layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"DEPTH = 3","new":"DEPTH = 4","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"Capacity increases have continued to produce small but real gains, and the current wide constant-width MLP is a good candidate for one additional hidden block. Increasing depth by one is a simple architecture extension that may improve representation power without changing the successful optimizer, batch size, or regularization settings.","secondary_modes":["caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"DEPTH = 4","new":"DEPTH = 3","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The extra hidden block hurt validation loss, so the best capacity tradeoff is still the three-layer variant. Reverting to depth 3 restores the stronger architecture before using the final step on a smaller follow-up tweak.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 2048","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 4096","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Batch scaling was the most consistently successful direction in this run, with each major increase improving validation loss until the current best regime. For the final step, pushing to 4096 is the clearest high-upside continuation of that trend while leaving the stronger wider architecture and optimizer settings intact.","secondary_modes":["layout"]}
